data.content:
Here are the benchmark results comparing .NET 9 vs .NET 10:

```
| Method              | Runtime   |     Mean |   Error |    StdDev | Ratio | Allocated |
|-------------------- |---------- |---------:|--------:|----------:|------:|----------:|
| AverageWordLength   | .NET 10.0 |  33.9 µs |  4.0 µs |  11.7 µs  |  0.92 |         - |
| AverageWordLength   | .NET 9.0  |  38.3 µs |  2.7 µs |   7.6 µs  |  1.04 |         - |
|                     |           |          |         |           |       |           |
| WordLengthHistogram | .NET 10.0 |  58.2 µs |  6.3 µs |  18.6 µs  |  0.78 |     104 B |
| WordLengthHistogram | .NET 9.0  |  75.5 µs |  2.6 µs |   7.2 µs  |  1.01 |     104 B |
|                     |           |          |         |           |       |           |
| UniqueWordCount     | .NET 10.0 | 464.8 µs | 43.9 µs | 126.1 µs  |  4.32 |    1553 B |
| UniqueWordCount     | .NET 9.0  | 107.6 µs |  2.1 µs |   3.1 µs  |  1.00 |    1552 B |
```

**Summary:**

- **AverageWordLength** — ~12% faster on .NET 10 (33.9 µs vs 38.3 µs), no allocations on either.
- **WordLengthHistogram** — ~23% faster on .NET 10 (58.2 µs vs 75.5 µs), same allocations.
- **UniqueWordCount** — ⚠️ ~4.3× **slower** on .NET 10 (464.8 µs vs 107.6 µs), with very high variance (StdDev 126 µs). BenchmarkDotNet flagged this as bimodal, suggesting the machine was under heavy load during that run. The identical allocation profile (1552–1553 B) indicates no algorithmic change — this is likely environmental noise rather than a real regression.

**Caveat:** BenchmarkDotNet reported multimodal distributions and outliers on all three .NET 10 runs, consistent with the machine being under load from other processes. The `UniqueWordCount` regression in particular should be re-validated on a quieter machine before drawing conclusions.
